coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-based molecularly imprinted polymer fluorescent sensor for the detection of propranolol in plasma
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jialin
Li, Ruizhen
Qin, Lei
Fu, Dongju
Wang, Moeiling
Liu, Weifeng
Liu, Xuguang
coredata.dc:description: Propranolol, a medication used to treat cardiovascular diseases, can be harmful when overdosed and hazardous to ecosystems if released into the environment. Here, a new molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor was developed from carbon dots through a sol–gel method. Carbon dots served as both the fluorescent signal and the carrier, with propranolol as the template molecule and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane as the functional monomer to be grafted on carbon dots’ surface. A novel detection method was established for the efficient, rapid, and cost-effective detection of propranolol in human plasma through quantitative analysis by using a fluorescence spectrophotometer and an ultraviolet spectrophotometer. Under the optimal conditions, the detection range of 0.5–4 mg L−1, the detection limit of 0.092 mg L−1, the imprinting factor of 2.42, and the detection response time of 2 min were achieved. The prepared carbon dot-based molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor was proved to have a wide accurate linear range, low detection limit, and very short response time, and can detect lower analyte concentrations with higher detection accuracy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Propranolol
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and properties of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs
Optimization of preparation and detection parameters

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis process of CDs; (b) Preparation process of CDs@MIPs; (c) Detection principle of CDs@MIPs.

(a–c) TEM images of CDs; (d) FT-IR spectra; (e) UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs; (f) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with addition of the indicated concentrations of PRO; (g) Linear relationship between the quenching efficiency of CDs and the concentration of PRO (pH = 7, T = 25 °C, t = 2 min, liquid).

(a) CDs@MIPs without eluting template molecules; (b, d, f) CDs@MIPs after eluting template molecules; (c, e) TEM images of CDs@NIPs; (g) FT-IR spectra of CDs, CDs@MIPs before and after eluting template molecules, and CDs@NIPs; (h) Localized zoomed-in images.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@NIPs, CDs@MIPs before and after elution of the template molecules, and CDs@MIPs after addition of 2 mg L −1 PRO; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs@MIPs and PRO; (c) Fluorescence intensities of CDs@MIPs preserved for 7 d at 4 °C; After addition of the indicated concentrations of PRO, (d) fluorescence spectra of CDs@ MIPs and (e) fluorescence spectra of CDs@NIPs; (f) Linear relationship between enhancement efficiency of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs with PRO concentration; Detectability of CDs@MIPs for PRO: (g) compared with its analogs and (h) coexisting with analogs (C CDs@MIPs = 1 mg mL−1, C PRO = C ACE = C MET = 2 mg L−1, V APTES = 100 μL, V TEOS = 450 μL, pH = 7, T = 25 °C, t = 2 min, liquid).
Effect of (a) APTES dosage, (b) TEOS dosage, (c) CDs@MIPs concentration, (d) pH, (e) time, and (f) temperature on CDs@MIPs detection performance (C CDs@MIPs = 1.0 mg mL−1, C PRO = 2 mg L−1, liquid).
Comparison of results of various methods for detecting PRO.
Results of quantitative determination of PRO in plasma.
Comparison of detection for PRO by using different materials.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot-based molecularly imprinted polymer fluorescent sensor for the detection of propranolol in plasma

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of New Carbon Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of New Carbon Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology), Ministry of Education, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology), Ministry of Education, Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of New Materials and New Energies, Shenzhen Technology University, Shenzhen 518118, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of New Materials and New Energies

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of New Materials and New Energies, Shenzhen Technology University, Shenzhen 518118, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The template molecule propranolol and the functional monomer 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane interacted via hydrogen bonding, and the copolymerization of 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane and ethyl n-silicate on the surface of the carbon dots catalyzed by ammonia resulted in the embedding of the template molecule into the silica-coated matrix. The unreacted reagents and template molecules were subsequently washed away to prepare carbon dot-based molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor.

Propranolol, a medication used to treat cardiovascular diseases, can be harmful when overdosed and hazardous to ecosystems if released into the environment. Here, a new molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor was developed from carbon dots through a sol–gel method. Carbon dots served as both the fluorescent signal and the carrier, with propranolol as the template molecule and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane as the functional monomer to be grafted on carbon dots’ surface. A novel detection method was established for the efficient, rapid, and cost-effective detection of propranolol in human plasma through quantitative analysis by using a fluorescence spectrophotometer and an ultraviolet spectrophotometer. Under the optimal conditions, the detection range of 0.5–4 mg L−1, the detection limit of 0.092 mg L−1, the imprinting factor of 2.42, and the detection response time of 2 min were achieved. The prepared carbon dot-based molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor was proved to have a wide accurate linear range, low detection limit, and very short response time, and can detect lower analyte concentrations with higher detection accuracy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Propranolol (PRO) is a β-blocker, known to prevent ischemia, lower blood pressure, treat cardiac arrhythmias, and prevent migraines [1–3], but high doses of PRO can adversely affect the cardiovascular system, causing confusion and depression [4,5]. In addition, PRO is frequently detected in the effluent of wastewater treatment plants, and when discharged into rivers, can contaminate surface and groundwater, causing harm to human health and pollution of the environment [6]. Various techniques are available for the detection of PROs, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [8], and electrochemical detection (ED) [9] (Table 1 ). Although these methods are mature, efficient, and accurate [10], they are also suffering from complex, time-consuming and costly operations [11]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop an efficient and rapid PRO detection method.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, which consist of surface functional groups and carbon cores. In recent years, CDs have been extensively used in the fields of chemical and biological sensing, bioimaging, fluorescent probes, and drug delivery, with outstanding advantages such as low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and excellent optical properties and stability [12–20]. CDs are modified by surface silanization to obtain silane-functionalized carbon dots (Si-CDs), which significantly reduce mass transfer resistance, form fine recognition sites and facilitate the grafting of polymers with a variety of functional groups onto the surface [21]. Si-CDs have high sensitivity for detecting organic or biomolecules with high sensitivity when colliding with the target molecule under UV light, with changes in fluorescence intensity and visual color [22], but are susceptible to interference from other substances and have poor selectivity.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) can be polymerized in the presence of template molecules, functional monomers, cross-linkers, and initiators [23]. After polymerization, the template molecules are easily removed from the rigid polymer network by suitable solvents, forming cavities with the same size, shape, and spatial structure as the template molecules [24]. Owing to their excellent stability, selectivity, low cost, and ease of preparation, MIPs are widely used in environmental monitoring, food testing, and drug testing. For example, Xie et al. [25] constructed a novel fluorescent molecular imprinted nanoplatform using glycopeptides as templates and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as substrates for efficient recognition and detection of glycoproteins. The Imprinting Factor was 7.71, the detection limit was 0.20 × 10−3 mg L−1, and the recovery reached 94.3 %–100.2 %. Liu et al. [26] combined CDs with restricted access materials and MIPs to prepare a highly sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor for the detection of metronidazole in serum, with a linear range of 0.050–1.20 mg L−1, a detection limit of 1.74 × 10−2 mg L−1, and recoveries of 93.5 %–102.7 %. Wang et al. [27] combined the fluorescence analysis technique with molecular imprinting to prepare a fluorescence sensor for copper ions, which showed good linearity in the range of 0.25–2 mg L−1 and 3–10 mg L−1 with recoveries of 99.29 %–105.42 %.

In this work, combining the sensitive fluorescence properties of CDs with the specific recognition of target molecules by MIPs, a unique CDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor was prepared by using Si-CDs as the carrier and fluorescent signal, PRO as the template molecule, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) as the functional monomer, and ethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) as the cross-linking agent to achieve efficient and rapid detection of PROs. The preparation and detection conditions of CDs@MIPs were optimized according to the changes in fluorescence intensity to maximize the detection range of PRO and the detection performance of CDs@MIPs.

Trisodium citrate dehydrate (C6H5Na3O7) was purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). N-(2-Aminoethyl)-3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (KH-792), propranolol (PRO), aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), sodium sulphate (Na2SO4), acetic acid (CH3COOH), acetaminophen (ACE), and metoprolol tartrate (MET) were purchased from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 99 %), dichloromethane (DCM, 99.5 %), and ethanol (C2H5OH, 99.7 %) were purchased from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Ammonia solution (25 %) was purchased from Tianjin Beichen Fangzheng Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Artificial synthetic blood was purchased from Phygene Life Sciences Co., Ltd (Fujian, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade. All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water.

The microstructure of the samples was analyzed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-2010, Japan). The surface functional groups of the samples were characterized with KBr pellet by Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR; BRUKER TENSOR 27, Germany) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. UV–visible (UV–vis) spectra were measured by a Jinghua 756MC UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded by using a Horiba Fluoromax-4 luminescence spectrometer.

According to the previous study [28], CDs were modified, with only a change in the silane coupling agent. Typically, 8 mmol of trisodium citrate was dissolved in 32 mL of ultrapure water under ultrasound in a one-step hydrothermal method, followed by the addition of 34 mmol of silane coupling agent (KH-792), and stirring for 15 min. The resulting precursor was sealed in a 100 mL hydrothermal high-pressure reactor, and then subjected to a hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 2 h to carbonize and functionalize the CDs. After cooling to room temperature, twice the volume of methanol was added to the product, and the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min and dissolved in ultrapure water. The obtained Si-CDs solution was collected and kept at 4 °C. The synthesis of Si-CDs is shown in Fig. 1 (a).

CDs@MIPs were prepared using the one-pot method [29]. First, 50 mg of PRO, 100 μL of APTES, and 15 mL of ethanol were added into a brown glass vial. The mixed solution was ultrasonicated for 5 min and stirred in a water bath at 25 °C for 30 min, and then 500 μL of CDs, 450 μL of TEOS, and 500 μL of ammonia (25 %) were added sequentially and reacted in a water bath at 60 °C for 24 h. The products were separated by centrifugation, and the resulting precipitates were washed by centrifugation with methanol/acetic acid (9:1, vol%) for several times to break the hydrogen bond between PRO and MIPs, and later dried at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain CDs@MIPs. The preparation process is shown in Fig. 1(b). Using the same preparation steps, CDs@NIPs were prepared without the addition of the template molecule PRO.

As shown in Fig. 1(c), APTES interacts with CDs in the form of hydrogen bonds during the preparation of CDs@MIPs, and CDs lose a certain amount of energy and the fluorescence intensity is reduced. When CDs@MIPs are used to detect PRO, with the addition of PRO, part of APTES interacts with PRO in the form of hydrogen bonding, the alkylamine group is passivated, and the non-radiative transitions in the detection system are reduced, which leads to the enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of CDs.

The CDs and CDs@MIPs (CDs@NIPs) obtained above were mixed with PRO solution, and fluorescence measurements were carried out at excitation and emission slit widths of 2.5 nm and 10 nm, excitation wavelength of 385 nm, and photomultiplier voltage of 400 V, respectively. The fluorescence enhancement efficiency is expressed as η, while 1 / η indicates the quenching efficiency.
(1)
η = F /
F 0
where F 0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity of the solution before and after the addition of PRO, respectively.

Artificial blood samples were mixed with acetonitrile and filtered, and the supernatant was removed and mixed with CDs@MIPs (CDs@NIPs) powder, and mixed with PRO, ACE, and MET, separately, and transferred to a volumetric flask to mix well. The fluorescence intensities of the three different combinations were measured under optimal test conditions and η was calculated.

Different concentrations (1, 2, 3, and 4 mg L −1) of PRO plasma standard solutions were prepared, and the spiked samples were detected and recovered by using CDs@MIPs, and the concentration of PRO was calculated from the linearity curve obtained from the test. The accuracy of this fluorescent sensor was evaluated by repeating the experiment four times under identical conditions and calculating the relative standard deviation (RSD). The rate of recovery is calculated by C 2 / C 1, where C 1 is the known concentration of PRO and C 2 is the concentration of PRO calculated by fitting a curve to the fluorescence intensity change.

The morphology and dispersion of the synthesized CDs were characterized by TEM. As can be seen in Fig. 2 (a–c), the CDs are spherical or hemispherical, with a diameter of about 2.5 nm and a lattice stripe spacing of 0.3004 nm, and well dispersed without obvious agglomeration.

Under hydrothermal reaction conditions, trisodium citrate undergoes an acylation reaction with KH-792 to form an amide bond, yielding CDs with carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups on the surface. The infrared spectra of the CDs were measured to determine the surface functional groups, as shown in Fig. 2(d). The large absorption band at 3223 cm−1 is attributed to the −O−H stretching vibration, demonstrating the presence of hydroxyl groups in the CDs. The shoulder band at 1684 cm−1 corresponds to the sp2 hybridization of −CO, indicating amide formation during the hydrothermal process. The band at 1574 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of −N−H for amino groups, indicating the presence of amino group. The sharp band at 1384 cm−1 corresponds to −C−H, 1063 cm−1 to the asymmetric stretching vibration of Si−O−Si, and 745 cm−1 to the Si−O vibration, demonstrating the synthesis of Si-CDs. In summary, the surface of Si-CDs is rich in carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups, which not only gives CDs excellent hydrophilicity and stability, but also allows them to interact with a variety of organic species.

As shown in Fig. 2(e), in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum, CDs exhibit distinct peaks in the range of 310−420 nm, with a characteristic broad absorption peak centered at 350 nm. This absorption peak is attributed to the π→π∗ electron transition of CC as well as the n→π* transition experienced by a large number of amide bonds attached to the carbon core surface [30]. The fluorescence excitation spectrum of CDs shows an optimal excitation centered at 385 nm the blue solid line. The fluorescence emission spectrum shows a strong peak at 442 nm at the optimal excitation wavelength the green solid line.

To investigate the sensitivity of CDs to PRO, different concentrations of PRO were added to the solution and the degree of quenching was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 2(f), the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 442 nm decreases gradually with the increase of PRO concentration, indicating that CDs are sensitive to PRO concentration.

In the range of PRO concentration of 0−12.5 mg L−1, the fluorescence quenching rate of CDs is linearly related to the concentration of PRO as shown in Eqs. (2) and (3), and the limit of detection (LOD) of PRO is 0.056 mg L−1.
(2)
1 / η = 1 +
K
SV
×
C
PRO
where K SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant.
(3)
LOD = 3 σ / k
where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample (n = 11) and k is the slope of the standard curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and properties of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs

ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 (a–c), CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs are spherical nanostructures with good dispersion, and CDs@MIPs keep their morphology before and after the template molecules are eluted. As seen in Fig. 3(d), the surface of CDs@MIPs prepared by sol–gel method is uniformly covered by a thick molecularly imprinted layer, which explains why the particle size of CDs@MIPs is larger than that of CDs@NIPs. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 3(e), the CDs@NIPs are spherical in shape, but since no template molecules were used in the fabrication process, their surfaces are smooth without a specifically identified molecularly imprinted cavity layer [31]. The size of the labelled CDs in Fig. 3(f) is less than 5 nm. Though they are hardly observed in the figure, their embedment in CDs@MIPs can be speculated.

According to Fig. 3(g), the band at 2974 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the −C−H single bond, the band at 1574 cm−1 indicates the bending vibration of −N−H, the band at 1384 cm−1 corresponds to the −COOH, and the band at 1158 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of −C−N. Owing to the structural similarity between CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs, their FT-IR spectra do not show significant differences. Compared with CDs, CDs@MIPs shows a new Si−O vibrational band at 794 cm−1, which indicates that TEOS participated in the synthesis process and displayed a strong cross-linking ability, further proving that MIPs were grafted onto the surface of CDs. In addition, the molecular imprinting process leads to no loss of the major functional groups of CDs and has no influence on the subsequent detection.

As shown in Fig. 3(h), the bands at 1569 cm−1 and 1577 cm−1 of CDs@MIPs before template elution are attributed to the naphthalene ring in PRO molecules. The characteristic bands disappear after template elution, suggesting that PRO molecules can be accurately encapsulated in CDs@MIPs and effectively removed by solvent elution. The FT-IR spectra of CDs@NIPs are similar to those of CDs@MIPs after template removal, suggesting that PRO acts only as a template and has no effect on other functional groups.

As shown in Fig. 4 (a), CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs show the same fluorescence emission peaks as CDs at 442 nm, indicating that the light emission mechanism of CDs is not changed after being embedded in CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs by crosslinking and polymerization with functional monomers, cross-linkers, and template molecules. Meanwhile, CDs@MIPs display intense fluorescence before elution, and the fluorescence intensity decreases after elution of the template, which is comparable to that of CDs@NIPs. After adding 2 mg L−1 PRO molecule, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIPs recovers again. This phenomenon can be explained by the charge transfer between CDs@MIPs and PRO molecules.

From Fig. 4(b), it can be seen that the characteristic absorption peaks of PRO in the range of 250−320 nm are absent in CDs@MIPs. This phenomenon can also indicate that the PRO in CDs@MIPs is eluted. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIPs was recorded continuously for 7 days, as shown in Fig. 4(c). The results demonstrate that the fluorescence intensity measured on the last day can maintain up to 95 % of its initial value in the first day, proving the good stability of the fluorescence sensor.

Fig. 4(d, e) shows the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs with increasing PRO concentration, respectively. Although PRO exhibits fluorescence enhancement for both CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIPs is much higher than that of CDs@NIPs with the increase of PRO concentration. According to Fig. 4(f), the detection limit of CDs@MIPs is 0.092 mg L−1, whereas that of CDs@NIPs is 0.276 mg L−1. Imprinting Factor (IF) is a parameter that describes the ability of CDs@MIPs to recognize template molecules and determines the selectivity of CDs@MIPs. An IF greater than 1.0 indicates that CDs@MIPs have certain selectivity to the target molecules; the larger the IF, the greater the selectivity, and the better the recognition effect. According to Eq. (4), IF = 2.42.

In summary, the sensitivity of CDs@MIPs to the target molecule PRO is much higher than that of CDs@NIPs, which is mainly attributed to the fact that there are specific recognition sites in CDs@MIPs that can selectively bind to PRO, whereas there is only physical adsorption of PRO by CDs@NIPs.
(4)
IF =
k
CDs @ M I P s
/
k
CDs @ N I P s
where k CDs@MIPs and k CDs@NIPs are the slopes of the standard curves for CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs with values of 0.02912 and 0.01204, respectively.

ACE and MTE, which are structurally similar to PRO and can also be present in plasma, are used to evaluate the selectivity and immunity of the CDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor. As shown in Fig. 4(g), the response of CDs@MIPs to PRO is much larger than those of ACE and MTE. The presence of imprinted cavities with the same structure and size as those of PRO in CDs@MIPs provides specific recognition of PRO. In contrast, the fluorescence response of CDs@NIPs to PRO is similar to that of interfering substances, proving the lack of selectivity. Detection of the above three drugs with CDs alone reveals high sensitivity but lack of specificity. Therefore, CDs@MIPs are suitable for selective detection of PRO in plasma.

As shown in Fig. 4(h), the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIPs does not change significantly when PRO and interfering substances coexist, demonstrating excellent anti-interference capability and the ability to accurately detect PRO in complex detection environments.

As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries range from 89.7 % to 107.8 % and the relative standard deviations RSD (n = 4) range from 0.89 % to 4.72 %. Thus, the CDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor is a promising tool for the trace detection of PRO in plasma samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of preparation and detection parameters

ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 5 (a), it can be seen that η first increases and then decreases with increasing APTES content. When the APTES dosage is 100 μL, η reaches the maximum value, indicating that the prepared CDs@MIPs have a better PRO detection effect at this APTES content. This is because when the APTES content is too low to fully react with the active sites on the surface of the CDs, fewer imprinted recognition cavities are formed. Besides, because the molecularly imprinted polymers have high cross-linking properties, with the increase of APTES content, the MIPs will undergo self-polymerization, and the generated imprinted cavities are not on or close to the surface of the material, resulting in the inability of PROs to enter the cavities, and the fluorescence enhancement effect will be weakened. Therefore, the optimal amount of functional monomer APTES was 100 μL.

As shown in Fig. 5(b), when all other conditions are kept constant, η shows an overall increasing and then decreasing trend with the increase of TEOS content. When the dosage is 450 μL, η reaches the maximum value. If the cross-linker content is higher than 450 μL, too much cross-linker may affect the accessibility of the recognition sites and the removal efficiency of the template molecules, while when the cross-linker content is lower than 450 μL, the nanoparticle structure may be too loose to form effective recognition sites. Therefore, the optimal dosage of TEOS is 450 μL.

As shown in Fig. 5(c), η increases and then decreases with the increase in the concentration of CDs@MIPs, reaching a maximum at a concentration of 1.0 mg mL−1. This is because passivation is a way to enhance the fluorescence intensity, and the alkylamine moiety of APTES induces a nonradiative transition of the excited electrons, that is, the excited electrons return to the ground state without producing photons, thus reducing the luminescence intensity of the CDs. In the presence of the template molecule, the non-radiative transitions are reduced due to the passivation of the alkylamine moiety by linking it to the template molecule, resulting in increased fluorescence intensity of the CDs. At a concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1, a portion of PRO is attached to APTES for alkylamines passivation, and another portion is used as a quencher for CDs. As the concentration of CDs@MIPs increases, more APTES is passivated and the fluorescence enhancement is increased. When the concentration of CDs@MIPs is too large, PRO is not enough to passivate more APTES, resulting in most APTES still undergoes non-radiative transitions, which leads to a weak fluorescence enhancement effect. Therefore, the detection effect is best when the concentration of CDs@MIPs is 1.0 mg mL−1.

In Fig. 5(d), when the detection solution is acidic, the fluorescence enhancement effect is not obvious. The reason is that at low pH, the hydrogen bonding strength between PRO and APTES is low, and it is difficult for PRO to enter the imprinted cavities to achieve the detection effect, so η is close to 1. When the detection solution is alkaline, η is also close to 1. This can be attributed to the fact that the unstable structure of PRO at high pH leads to no effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIPs. In summary, the detection effect is best when the pH of the detection solution is 7.

As shown in Fig. 5(e), the fluorescence response of CDs@MIPs reaches equilibrium only 2 min after the addition of a certain amount of PRO, which is resulted from the large number of effective recognition sites and the thorough elution of template molecules [31]. As shown in Table 3 , the CDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor has a lower detection limit and a shorter response time than other PRO detection methods. Therefore, this PRO fluorescent sensor has the advantages of high efficiency and time saving.

According to Fig. 5(f), the fluorescence intensity shows an increasing and then decreasing tendency with rising temperature, and reaches the maximum value at 25 °C. At low temperature, molecules move slowly and PRO cannot enter the imprinted cavity within 2 min, so the fluorescence enhancement effect is weak. With the increase of temperature, the molecular movement is enhanced, the spatial resistance is reduced, and PRO can enter the imprinted cavity more easily to achieve the fluorescence enhancement effect. As the detection temperature is further increased, the contracted pores in CDs@MIPs matrix enlarge, exposing cavities that are unusable at room temperature, and PRO is insufficient to passivate the APTES around the cavity. Consequently, the non-radiative transition reduces the fluorescence intensity of the CDs, leading to a weaker fluorescence enhancement effect of PRO on the CDs@MIPs [32]. Therefore, 25 °C is the ideal detection temperature.

In this work, a highly efficient, rapid, and cost-effective method for the detection of PRO in human plasma is established by using carbon dot-based molecularly imprinted polymers (CDs@MIPs) in combination with fluorescence spectrophotometry, and the preparation and detection conditions are optimized. This fluorescent sensor CDs@MIPs has the advantages of both unique optical properties and high sensitivity of CDs and the specific recognition of MIPs, which can realize the detection of PRO in artificial plasma within 2 min. The linear range of detection is 0.5–4 mg L−1, and the detection limit is 0.092 mg L−1. The CDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor is used for the detection of PRO in artificial plasma with good anti-interference performance and the recoveries of PRO ranged from 89.7 % to 107.8 %. Therefore, CDs@MIPs is a promising method for trace detection of PRO in plasma samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Central Leading Science and Technology Development Foundation of Shanxi Province (YDZJSX2022A009), Innovation Project of Education Department of Guangdong Province (2023KTSCX124), Shenzhen Science and Technology Program (20231127203830001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125590.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
